1	# Minor-Piece Endgames Overview (Bishop vs Knight)
2	
3	> A class of endgames where one side has a bishop and the other a knight (with pawns), and the winner is usually decided by pawn structure, open lines, and target squares rather than material.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1600–2200 FIDE
6	**Prerequisites:** [King and Pawn Endgames](king-pawn-endgames.md), [The Opposition](opposition.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	A bishop and a knight are both worth roughly three pawns, but they move in completely different ways. The bishop is a long-range piece that controls squares of one color across the whole board; the knight is short-range but can reach both colors and jump over pawns. Who is better depends almost entirely on the position.
11	
12	The single most useful rule of thumb: **the bishop is stronger in open positions with pawns on both wings**, because it can defend one flank and attack the other in a single move. The **knight is stronger in closed, blocked positions**, especially when it has a protected outpost and the bishop is hemmed in by its own fixed pawns (a "bad bishop").
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	These endings appear constantly — most middlegames simplify into a single minor piece each. Knowing which type of position favors your piece tells you *what to aim for* twenty moves earlier: with the bishop you trade to open the position and create play on both flanks; with the knight you fix the pawns, block the diagonals, and plant a knight on an outpost. Getting this evaluation right is worth more rating points than memorizing any single line.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . . . . .
22	7 . . . . . k . .
23	6 . . . . . . . .
24	5 p . . . . . . p
25	4 . . . . . . . .
26	3 P . . . . . . P
27	2 . . . . B . . .
28	1 . . . . . K . .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*The good bishop: pawns on both wings, open diagonals — the bishop touches a5 and h5 at once.*
32	**FEN:** 8/5k2/8/p6p/8/P6P/4B3/5K2 w - - 0 1
33	Side to move: White.
34	[Analyse on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/5k2/8/p6p/8/P6P/4B3/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)
35	
36	The bishop on e2 can swing to a6 or to d1–h5 and pressure both `a5` and `h5` while the king strolls forward. A knight here would need many tempi to switch wings, so this open, two-flank structure is the bishop's ideal habitat.
37	
38	```
39	8 . . . . . . . .
40	7 . . . . . k . .
41	6 . . . p . . . .
42	5 . . . P . N . .
43	4 . . . . p . . .
44	3 . . . . P b . .
45	2 . . . . . . . .
46	1 . . . . . K . .
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*The good knight vs bad bishop: pawns fixed on light squares, knight on a dark outpost, bishop biting on granite.*
50	**FEN:** 8/5k2/3p4/3P1N2/4p3/4Pb2/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
51	Side to move: White.
52	[Analyse on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/5k2/3p4/3P1N2/4p3/4Pb2/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)
53	
54	The black bishop on f3 is "bad": its own pawns on d6 and e4 sit on its color and block it, while the white knight on f5 sits on a dark outpost it can never be chased from by a pawn. In closed structures like this the knight quietly dominates.
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Open position, two wings:** bishop is clearly better; it shepherds a passer on one flank while holding the other.
59	- **Closed/fixed pawns + outpost:** knight is better; the bishop is reduced to a passive defender.
60	- **Bad bishop:** a bishop blocked by its own pawns fixed on its color — strategically often worse than the knight.
61	- **Good knight:** a knight on a hole that no enemy pawn can attack, ideally supported by a pawn.
62	- **Same-color vs opposite emphasis:** with a bishop, keep your pawns on the *opposite* color to the bishop so they aren't blocked and the bishop stays mobile.
63	- **Wrong-bishop draw:** a rook-pawn plus a bishop that does **not** control the promotion square is only a draw if the lone king reaches the corner.
64	
65	## How to spot it
66	
67	```mermaid
68	flowchart TD
69	  A[Minor piece each] --> B{Pawns on both wings}
70	  B -->|Yes and open| C[Favor the bishop]
71	  B -->|No, one wing| D{Structure closed}
72	  D -->|Yes fixed pawns| E[Favor the knight]
73	  D -->|No, open| C
74	  E --> F{Knight has outpost}
75	  F -->|Yes| G[Knight clearly better]
76	  F -->|No| H[Roughly equal]
77	  C --> I{Bishop blocked by own pawns}
78	  I -->|Yes| H
79	  I -->|No| J[Bishop clearly better]
80	```
81	
82	## Worked examples
83	
84	### Example 1 — the wrong-bishop draw
85	
86	```
87	8 . . . . . . k .
88	7 . . . . . . . .
89	6 . . . . . . K .
90	5 . . . . . . P .
91	4 . . . . . . . B
92	3 . . . . . . . .
93	2 . . . . . . . .
94	1 . . . . . . . .
95	  a b c d e f g h
96	```
97	*White has bishop + g-pawn but the bishop is the wrong color for the h8 corner — Black draws by reaching the corner.*
98	**FEN:** 6k1/8/6K1/6P1/7B/8/8/8 b - - 0 1
99	Side to move: Black.
100	[Analyse on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/8/6K1/6P1/7B/8/8/8_b_-_-_0_1)
101	
102	This is a g-pawn, and after it promotes the queening square is g8 — a light square. But imagine the pawn were an h-pawn with a dark-squared bishop: the corner h8 is dark only when the bishop is light. The classic drawing rule: **rook-pawn + bishop not controlling the promotion-corner square = draw** if the defending king reaches that corner. Here, to make it a true wrong-bishop, picture an h-pawn with this same bishop (which controls g8, not h8): Black plays `1...Kh8!` and can never be driven out — stalemate ideas save him. *Lesson: before trading into B+RP, check the corner color.*
103	
104	### Example 2 — knight outplays bishop in a closed position
105	
106	```
107	8 . . . . . . . .
108	7 . . . . . k . .
109	6 . . . p . p . .
110	5 . . . P . P . .
111	4 . . . . . . b .
112	3 . . . . . N . .
113	2 . . . . . K . .
114	1 . . . . . . . .
115	  a b c d e f g h
116	```
117	*Fixed pawns, knight maneuvers to a dominant square while the bishop has no targets.*
118	**FEN:** 8/5k2/3p1p2/3P1P2/6b1/5N2/5K2/8 w - - 0 1
119	Side to move: White.
120	[Analyse on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/5k2/3p1p2/3P1P2/6b1/5N2/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)
121	
122	The pawn chain is frozen. White's plan is to route the knight to e6 or c6 (via `Nf3–d4–e6`) where it forks the f-pawn and dark squares and the bishop cannot challenge it. The bishop on g4 has nothing to attack — every black pawn sits on a square it cannot reach. The knight's flexibility on both colors decides the game.
123	
124	## Common mistakes
125	
126	- Trading into a bishop ending and then **leaving your own pawns on the bishop's color**, turning a good bishop into a bad one.
127	- Keeping the position closed when you have the **bishop** — open it up so the long-range piece dominates.
128	- Opening the position when you have the **knight** — you want it closed and fixed.
129	- Forgetting the **wrong-bishop** rule and trading into a "winning" rook-pawn ending that is actually drawn.
130	- Putting the knight on a square with **no outpost**, where it can be chased by pawns and the bishop reigns.
131	- Underestimating the bishop's power to **defend two wings at once** — don't create weaknesses on both flanks against a bishop.
132	
133	## Train it
134	
135	- **Bishop vs knight technique:** play out the two diagram structures above vs an engine, 10 reps each, until the winning/holding method is automatic.
136	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train the `endgame`, `bishopEndgame`, and `knightEndgame` themes, 20 puzzles per session.
137	- **Wrong-bishop:** set up B + rook-pawn vs lone king from both corner colors and draw/win 5 times each from memory.
138	- **Books:** Dvoretsky, *Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual* (chapters on bishop vs knight); Silman, *Silman's Complete Endgame Course*; Flear, *Practical Endgame Play – Mastering the Critical Skills* for the minor-piece chapters.
139	- **Model games:** study Fischer–Taimanov, Candidates 1971 (bishop dominating knight on both wings) and Karpov's many "good knight" wins.
140	
141	## Exercises
142	
143	1. **FEN:** `8/5k2/8/p6p/8/P6P/4B3/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Which wing should White's bishop and king target first, and why?
144	2. **FEN:** `8/5k2/3p1p2/3P1P2/6b1/5N2/5K2/8 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the knight route to a dominating square.
145	3. **FEN:** `6k1/8/5K2/7P/7B/8/8/8 w - - 0 1` — White to move (h-pawn, light bishop). Is this won or drawn? State the rule.
146	4. **FEN:** `8/8/4k3/8/3KP3/8/2b5/8 w - - 0 1` — White to move. With a single passed pawn and bishop vs king, what is the simple evaluation?
147	
148	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
149	
150	1. The bishop attacks both `a5` and `h5` from one diagonal, so White advances the king toward whichever pawn is harder to defend while the bishop ties Black down on the other wing. The two-wing, open structure is winning for the bishop side because it operates on both flanks in one tempo.
151	2. `1.Nd4` heading for `e6` (or `c6`): from `e6` the knight hits the f6-pawn and dominates dark squares the bishop can never cover. Black's bishop has no targets among the fixed pawns, so White wins material or zugzwangs Black.
152	3. **Drawn.** It is a rook-pawn (h-pawn) and the bishop is the *wrong color* — it controls `g8`, not the `h8` promotion square. Black draws by reaching the h8 corner: the king can never be evicted. Rule: rook-pawn + bishop not controlling the queening corner = draw if the defender reaches the corner.
153	4. This is just K+P+B vs K — trivially winning; the bishop is irrelevant material surplus. The point of the drill is to recognize that B + any non-rook pawn (or correct-color rook-pawn) is a routine win, and to contrast it with the wrong-bishop exception in #3.
154	
155	</details>
156	
157	## Related
158	
159	- [King and Pawn Endgames](king-pawn-endgames.md)
160	- [The Opposition](opposition.md)
161	- [Good Bishop vs Bad Bishop](good-bad-bishop.md)
162	- [Knight Outposts](knight-outposts.md)
163	- [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md)
164	
165	Concept tags: [[good-bad-bishop]] · [[knight-outposts]] · [[wrong-bishop]] · [[zugzwang]]

98	**FEN:** 7k/8/5K1P/3B4/8/8/8/8 b - - 0 1
99	Side to move: Black.
100	[Analyse on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/5K1P/3B4/8/8/8/8_b_-_-_0_1)
101	
102	This is an h-pawn, and its queening square h8 is a **dark** square. The light-squared bishop on d5 can never control h8 or cover g7 to shepherd the pawn home. Black simply oscillates `1...Kg8 2.h7+ Kh8` and after `3.Kg6` it is **stalemate** — the king can never be evicted from the corner. The classic drawing rule: **rook-pawn + bishop not controlling the promotion-corner square = draw** if the defending king reaches that corner. *Lesson: before trading into B+RP, check the corner color.*
